title: Count Distinct Substrings in a String
description: Count total number of unique substrings

examples.explanation: String length n=3. Initialize distinct=set(). Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: substring s[0:1]="a", distinct={"a"}. j=1: substring s[0:2]="ab", distinct={"a","ab"}. j=2: substring s[0:3]="abc", distinct={"a","ab","abc"}. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: substring s[1:2]="b", distinct={"a","ab","abc","b"}. j=2: substring s[1:3]="bc", distinct={"a","ab","abc","b","bc"}. Outer loop i=2: inner loop j=2: substring s[2:3]="c", distinct={"a","ab","abc","b","bc","c"}. Loop ends. Return len(distinct)=6.

String length n=3. Initialize distinct=set(). Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: substring s[0:1]="a", distinct={"a"}. j=1: substring s[0:2]="aa", distinct={"a","aa"}. j=2: substring s[0:3]="aaa", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"}. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: substring s[1:2]="a", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"} (duplicate not added). j=2: substring s[1:3]="aa", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"} (duplicate not added). Outer loop i=2: inner loop j=2: substring s[2:3]="a", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"} (duplicate not added). Loop ends. Return len(distinct)=3.

approaches.name: Brute Force with HashSet O(n²)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible substrings using nested loops and store them in a HashSet to count unique ones. This is straightforward but inefficient for large strings due to quadratic time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Substrings in a String using Brute Force with HashSet
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to count all unique substrings by generating all possible substrings and storing them in a HashSet.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to counting distinct substrings is conceptually straightforward but computationally expensive. The intuition is that every substring can be uniquely identified by its starting and ending indices. By systematically generating all possible substrings through nested loops, we can collect them in a HashSet which automatically handles duplicates. This works because a HashSet stores only unique elements, so its size after adding all substrings gives us the count of distinct ones. The algorithm begins by initializing an empty HashSet. The outer loop iterates over each possible starting index i from 0 to n-1 (where n is the string length). For each i, the inner loop iterates over each possible ending index j from i to n-1. In each inner iteration, we extract the substring from i to j+1 (since Python slicing is exclusive at the end) and add it to the HashSet. After all loops complete, the size of the HashSet is returned as the count of distinct substrings. The time complexity is O(n²) because there are n starting positions, and for each starting position, there can be up to n ending positions, resulting in n(n+1)/2 ≈ n² substrings generated. Each substring operation takes O(k) time where k is the substring length, but the total work sums to O(n³) for substring creation. However, with efficient string handling (like Python's slicing which creates new strings in O(k)), the dominant factor becomes O(n²) operations with varying costs. The space complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case (when all substrings are unique, like in a string with all distinct characters), we store all n(n+1)/2 substrings, each of which can be up to n characters long. This approach connects directly to the problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries (e.g., using j <= n instead of j < n), forgetting to handle empty strings (which should return 0 or 1 depending on whether the empty substring is counted—typically it's not counted in this problem), and not using an efficient data structure for uniqueness, leading to O(n³) time if checking duplicates in a list. To avoid these, carefully define substring indices and use HashSet for O(1) average-time insertions and uniqueness checks. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_distinct_substrings(s): n = len(s); distinct = set(); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): distinct.add(s[i:j+1]); return len(distinct).

examples.trace: String length n=3. Initialize distinct=set(). Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: substring s[0:1]="a", distinct={"a"}. j=1: substring s[0:2]="ab", distinct={"a","ab"}. j=2: substring s[0:3]="abc", distinct={"a","ab","abc"}. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: substring s[1:2]="b", distinct={"a","ab","abc","b"}. j=2: substring s[1:3]="bc", distinct={"a","ab","abc","b","bc"}. Outer loop i=2: inner loop j=2: substring s[2:3]="c", distinct={"a","ab","abc","b","bc","c"}. Loop ends. Return len(distinct)=6.

String length n=3. Initialize distinct=set(). Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: substring s[0:1]="a", distinct={"a"}. j=1: substring s[0:2]="aa", distinct={"a","aa"}. j=2: substring s[0:3]="aaa", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"}. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: substring s[1:2]="a", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"} (duplicate not added). j=2: substring s[1:3]="aa", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"} (duplicate not added). Outer loop i=2: inner loop j=2: substring s[2:3]="a", distinct={"a","aa","aaa"} (duplicate not added). Loop ends. Return len(distinct)=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All Substrings with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Implement the nested loop structure to generate every possible substring of a given string.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core engine of the brute force approach, responsible for systematically producing all substrings. The intuition is that any substring can be defined by two indices: a start index i and an end index j (inclusive). By varying i from 0 to n-1 and for each i, varying j from i to n-1, we cover all possible combinations without repetition. This works because it enumerates all pairs (i,j) where i ≤ j, which corresponds exactly to all substrings. The algorithm involves initializing an empty list or set to store substrings. Then, using an outer loop for i in range(n) and an inner loop for j in range(i, n), we extract the substring from i to j+1 (using slicing) and add it to the collection. The time complexity is O(n²) for the loop iterations, but note that substring extraction itself takes O(k) time per substring, where k is the length j-i+1. The total time for generating all substrings sums to O(n³) due to the cost of creating each substring, though in practice with efficient string operations, it's often considered O(n²) dominant. The space complexity is O(n²) if storing all substrings, as there are n(n+1)/2 of them. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by providing the list of substrings that will then be deduplicated in the HashSet. Without this generation, we cannot count distinct substrings. Common mistakes include incorrect loop ranges (e.g., starting j from 0 instead of i, leading to duplicate substrings or extra work), off-by-one errors in slicing (e.g., using s[i:j] instead of s[i:j+1]), and not handling empty strings properly. To avoid these, visualize the substring indices: for a string of length n, valid i values are 0 to n-1, and for each i, valid j values are i to n-1. Real code pattern: def generate_all_substrings(s): n=len(s); substrings=[]; for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substrings.append(s[i:j+1]); return substrings.

examples.trace: String length n=2. Initialize substrings=[]. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: substring s[0:1]="a", substrings=["a"]. j=1: substring s[0:2]="ab", substrings=["a","ab"]. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: substring s[1:2]="b", substrings=["a","ab","b"]. Loop ends. Return substrings.

String length n=3. Initialize substrings=[]. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: substring s[0:1]="x", substrings=["x"]. j=1: substring s[0:2]="xy", substrings=["x","xy"]. j=2: substring s[0:3]="xyz", substrings=["x","xy","xyz"]. Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: substring s[1:2]="y", substrings=["x","xy","xyz","y"]. j=2: substring s[1:3]="yz", substrings=["x","xy","xyz","y","yz"]. Outer loop i=2: inner loop j=2: substring s[2:3]="z", substrings=["x","xy","xyz","y","yz","z"]. Loop ends. Return substrings.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using HashSet for Uniqueness in Substring Collection
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to collect substrings in a HashSet to automatically remove duplicates and count unique ones.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of this approach is leveraging a HashSet data structure to efficiently manage uniqueness while counting substrings. The intuition is that a HashSet stores only distinct elements, so by adding each generated substring to it, duplicates are automatically ignored, and the final size gives the count of unique substrings. This works because HashSets use hashing to provide average O(1) time for insertions and lookups, making the deduplication process efficient compared to checking against a list which would be O(n) per operation. The algorithm involves initializing an empty HashSet. As substrings are generated (e.g., from nested loops), each substring is added to the set using the add operation. If the substring is already present, the add operation does nothing, ensuring no duplicates. After all substrings are processed, the size of the set is returned. The time complexity for adding m substrings to a HashSet is O(m) on average, assuming good hash distribution. In our case, m = n(n+1)/2, so this adds to the overall O(n²) complexity. The space complexity is O(u) where u is the number of unique substrings, which in worst case is O(n²). This logic is central to the full problem because it transforms the list of all substrings into a count of distinct ones. Without it, we would need manual duplicate checking, which is less efficient. Common mistakes include using a list instead of a set (leading to incorrect counts if duplicates are not handled), not initializing the set properly, or misunderstanding that sets are unordered (which doesn't matter for counting). To avoid these, always use a set for uniqueness tasks and understand that sets do not allow duplicate elements. Real code pattern: distinct = set(); for substring in all_substrings: distinct.add(substring); return len(distinct).

examples.trace: Initialize distinct=set(). Process 'a': distinct.add('a') -> distinct={'a'}. Process 'ab': distinct.add('ab') -> distinct={'a','ab'}. Process 'a': distinct.add('a') -> 'a' already in set, no change, distinct={'a','ab'}. Process 'b': distinct.add('b') -> distinct={'a','ab','b'}. All processed. Return len(distinct)=3.

Initialize distinct=set(). Process 'x': distinct.add('x') -> distinct={'x'}. Process 'xy': distinct.add('xy') -> distinct={'x','xy'}. Process 'x': distinct.add('x') -> duplicate, no change, distinct={'x','xy'}. Process 'y': distinct.add('y') -> distinct={'x','xy','y'}. Process 'xy': distinct.add('xy') -> duplicate, no change, distinct={'x','xy','y'}. All processed. Return len(distinct)=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over String Indices with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Implement nested loops to iterate over all possible start and end indices for substrings in a string.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the loop structure needed to traverse all substring indices without generating the substrings themselves. The intuition is that substrings are defined by contiguous sequences of characters, which can be represented by pairs of indices (i,j) where i is the start and j is the end (inclusive). By using an outer loop for the start index i and an inner loop for the end index j, we systematically cover all such pairs. This works because for a string of length n, i ranges from 0 to n-1, and for each i, j ranges from i to n-1, ensuring we consider every possible contiguous segment. The algorithm involves setting n = len(s). Then, for i from 0 to n-1, and for each i, for j from i to n-1, we can perform an operation (like printing or storing the indices). The time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops, as the total number of iterations is n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2. The space complexity is O(1) if we only store indices. This block feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the framework to which substring extraction is added. Without mastering this iteration, generating substrings would be haphazard. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., using j in range(i+1, n) which misses single-character substrings, or using j in range(0, n) which generates non-contiguous or duplicate sequences), and off-by-one errors with string length. To avoid these, remember that i and j are indices, so valid i values are 0 to n-1, and for each i, j starts at i and goes up to n-1. Visualize a triangle of indices. Real code pattern: n = len(s); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): # i and j are the start and end indices.

examples.trace: String length n=2. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: pair (0,0). j=1: pair (0,1). Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: pair (1,1). Loop ends. Output all pairs.

String length n=3. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=0: pair (0,0). j=1: pair (0,1). j=2: pair (0,2). Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=1: pair (1,1). j=2: pair (1,2). Outer loop i=2: inner loop j=2: pair (2,2). Loop ends. Output all pairs.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Extracting a Substring Given Start and End Indices
ladders.desc: Implement the operation to extract a substring from a string using start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves slicing a string to obtain a contiguous subsequence between two indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access a portion by specifying the start index (inclusive) and end index (exclusive in many programming languages). This works because strings support slicing operations that create a new string containing characters from the start index up to but not including the end index. For example, in Python, s[i:j] returns characters from index i to j-1. To include the character at index j, we use s[i:j+1]. The algorithm is straightforward: given a string s, start index i, and end index j (inclusive), return s[i:j+1]. The time complexity is O(k) where k = j-i+1 is the length of the substring, as it may need to copy characters to a new string. The space complexity is O(k) for the new substring. This operation feeds into the building block by being the action performed inside the nested loops to actually get the substring from the indices. Without this, we would only have indices without the actual substrings. Common mistakes include using incorrect slicing bounds (e.g., s[i:j] when j is inclusive, leading to off-by-one errors), not handling indices out of bounds (which should be avoided by proper loop control), and misunderstanding that slicing creates a new string (not a view). To avoid these, always remember the slicing semantics: s[start:end] gives characters from start to end-1. If end is inclusive, use end+1. Real code pattern: def extract_substring(s, i, j): return s[i:j+1].

examples.trace: String s="hello", indices i=1, j=3 (inclusive). Compute substring: s[1:4] because j+1=4. s[1:4] extracts characters at indices 1,2,3: 'e','l','l' -> "ell". Return "ell".
String s="abcde", indices i=0, j=4 (inclusive). Compute substring: s[0:5] because j+1=5. s[0:5] extracts characters at indices 0,1,2,3,4: 'a','b','c','d','e' -> "abcde". Return "abcde".

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Indexed Sequences
ladders.desc: Learn how strings are stored and accessed using indices in programming.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces strings as sequences of characters where each character has a position index. The intuition is that in programming, strings are often treated like arrays or lists, allowing access to individual characters via numeric indices starting from 0. This works because strings are immutable sequences in many languages, meaning they can be read but not modified directly, and indexing provides a way to retrieve characters. For example, in a string s = "cat", s[0] returns 'c', s[1] returns 'a', and s[2] returns 't'. The algorithm for accessing is simple: use square brackets with the index, e.g., s[i] to get the character at position i. The time complexity for index access is typically O(1) as it involves direct memory lookup. The space complexity is O(1) for the character retrieved. This concept feeds into the basic operation by enabling the extraction of substrings through slicing, which builds upon individual character access. Without understanding indices, operations like substring generation would be confusing. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking s[1] is the first character), going out of bounds (e.g., accessing s[3] in "cat" which has length 3, so valid indices are 0,1,2), and not realizing that strings are immutable (so s[0] = 'd' would cause an error in languages like Python). To avoid these, remember that indexing starts at 0, and the last index is length-1. Always check the length of the string before accessing. Real code pattern: s = "hello"; first_char = s[0]; # 'h'.

examples.trace: String s="dog" has length 3. Access s[0]: character at index 0 is 'd'. Access s[1]: character at index 1 is 'o'. Access s[2]: character at index 2 is 'g'. Output these characters.
String s="apple" has length 5. Access s[0]: character at index 0 is 'a'. Access s[2]: character at index 2 is 'p'. Access s[4]: character at index 4 is 'e'. Output these characters.

approaches.name: Trie-Based O(n²)
approaches.summary: Insert all suffixes of the string into a trie, where each node represents a unique substring. The total number of nodes in the trie gives the count of distinct substrings. This avoids explicit storage of all substrings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Substrings Using Trie
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all unique substrings by inserting all suffixes into a trie.
ladders.explanation: The full solution leverages the insight that every substring of a string S is a prefix of some suffix of S. By inserting all suffixes into a trie, each node in the trie corresponds to a unique substring. The total number of nodes (excluding the root) equals the count of distinct substrings. This approach avoids storing substrings explicitly, using the trie's structure to deduplicate efficiently. Intuitively, the trie compresses common prefixes, ensuring each distinct substring is counted exactly once. The algorithm iterates through each starting index i from 0 to n-1, treating S[i:] as a suffix. For each suffix, it traverses the trie, creating new nodes for characters not yet present. The total nodes created across all insertions give the distinct substring count. Time complexity is O(n²) in the worst case (e.g., all characters distinct), as each suffix insertion can take O(n) time, and there are n suffixes. Space complexity is O(n²) due to storing up to n(n+1)/2 nodes in the worst case. This connects to L1 by using the suffix insertion subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include forgetting to count the root node (which doesn't represent a substring) or mishandling empty strings. Real code pattern: Initialize a root node and count=0. For each i, set current=root, and for each character in suffix, check if child exists; if not, create it and increment count. Return count.

examples.trace: String: 'abc', length=3. Initialize trie root, count=0. Suffixes: 'abc', 'bc', 'c'. Insert 'abc': Start at root. 'a' not in root's children, create node for 'a', count=1. Move to 'a', 'b' not in children, create node for 'b', count=2. Move to 'b', 'c' not in children, create node for 'c', count=3. Insert 'bc': Start at root. 'b' not in root's children, create node for 'b', count=4. Move to 'b', 'c' not in children, create node for 'c', count=5. Insert 'c': Start at root. 'c' not in root's children, create node for 'c', count=6. Total nodes=6, distinct substrings: 'a', 'ab', 'abc', 'b', 'bc', 'c'.

String: 'aaa', length=3. Initialize trie root, count=0. Suffixes: 'aaa', 'aa', 'a'. Insert 'aaa': Start at root. 'a' not in root's children, create node for 'a', count=1. Move to 'a', 'a' not in children (first 'a' node has no child 'a'), create node for 'a', count=2. Move to new 'a', 'a' not in children, create node for 'a', count=3. Insert 'aa': Start at root. 'a' exists, move to 'a'. 'a' exists (from first insertion), move to 'a'. No further insertion as suffix ends. Insert 'a': Start at root. 'a' exists, move to 'a'. No further insertion. Total nodes=3, distinct substrings: 'a', 'aa', 'aaa'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert a Suffix into Trie
ladders.desc: Insert a single suffix string into a trie, creating nodes for new prefixes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation for building the trie. Given a suffix (e.g., S[i:]), it traverses the trie from the root, following child nodes corresponding to each character. If a character is not present as a child of the current node, a new node is created, representing a new unique substring prefix. The intuition is that each insertion adds nodes only for substrings not already in the trie, leveraging the trie's prefix-sharing property. The algorithm starts at the root node. For each character c in the suffix, check if c exists as a child of the current node. If not, create a new node for c and link it. Then, move to that node (new or existing). Continue until all characters are processed. This ensures that all prefixes of the suffix are accounted for in the trie. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the suffix, as each character requires a constant-time lookup/insertion. Space complexity is O(m) in the worst case if all characters are new nodes. This feeds into L0 by being called for each suffix to build the complete trie. Common mistakes include not moving to the child node after creation or incorrectly handling the end of the suffix. Real code pattern: Function insert_suffix(root, suffix): current = root; for char in suffix: if char not in current.children: create new node; current.children[char] = new node; current = current.children[char]; return number of new nodes created (optional).

examples.trace: Start with root node (no children). Process 'b': 'b' not in root's children, create node for 'b', count=1. Move to 'b' node. Process 'c': 'c' not in 'b' node's children, create node for 'c', count=2. Trie now has root -> 'b' -> 'c'.

examples.input: Suffix: 'ab', Trie has 'a' node from previous insertions
examples.trace: Trie initially: root -> 'a'. Start at root. Process 'a': 'a' exists as child, move to 'a' node. Process 'b': 'b' not in 'a' node's children, create node for 'b', count=1. Trie now: root -> 'a' -> 'b'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trie Node Representation and Counting
ladders.desc: Understand how trie nodes map to unique substrings and how to count them.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that in a trie built from suffixes, each node (except the root) corresponds to a unique substring of the original string. Specifically, the path from the root to a node spells out a substring that is a prefix of some suffix. Since the trie merges common prefixes, duplicate substrings are represented by the same node, ensuring uniqueness. The count of distinct substrings is simply the total number of nodes in the trie (excluding the root). Intuition: Every time we create a new node during suffix insertion, we've encountered a substring not seen before. The algorithm involves maintaining a trie structure where each node has children mapping characters to other nodes. As we insert suffixes, we increment a counter whenever a new node is created. This counter accumulates across all insertions to give the final count. Time and space analysis are tied to the number of nodes, which can be up to O(n²) in the worst case. This connects to L1 by providing the structural basis for the insertion subroutine. Common mistakes include counting the root node or missing nodes due to incorrect traversal. Real code pattern: Define a TrieNode class with a children dictionary. Initialize root = TrieNode() and count = 0. During insertion, for each new node created, increment count. After all insertions, count holds the answer.

examples.input: String: 'ab', build trie from suffixes 'ab' and 'b'
examples.trace: Insert 'ab': Create nodes for 'a' and 'b' (path root->'a'->'b'), count=2. Insert 'b': Start at root, 'b' not present, create node for 'b', count=3. Nodes: root (not counted), 'a', 'b' under 'a', and 'b' under root. Substrings: 'a', 'ab', 'b'.

examples.input: String: 'aa', build trie from suffixes 'aa' and 'a'
examples.trace: Insert 'aa': Create nodes for 'a' and then another 'a' (root->'a'->'a'), count=2. Insert 'a': 'a' exists under root, no new nodes. Nodes: root, 'a', 'a' under 'a'. Substrings: 'a', 'aa'.

testCases.input: Trie with single long chain of 10 nodes

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Suffixes of a String
ladders.desc: Extract all suffix strings from a given string for trie insertion.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves generating all suffixes of a string S of length n. A suffix is a substring that starts at some index i and goes to the end of the string, i.e., S[i:] for i from 0 to n-1. The intuition is that by processing all suffixes, we cover every possible substring, since any substring S[j:k] is a prefix of the suffix starting at j. The algorithm is straightforward: iterate i from 0 to n-1, and for each i, take the substring from i to the end. This can be done efficiently in O(n²) time total if substrings are extracted explicitly, but in the trie approach, we process them on-the-fly without storing all suffixes separately. Time complexity for generation is O(n²) in the worst case if we consider the total length of all suffixes, which is n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2. Space complexity is O(1) extra if not stored, or O(n²) if stored. This feeds into L2 by providing the input suffixes for trie insertion. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or missing the empty suffix (which is not needed here). Real code pattern: For i in range(len(S)): suffix = S[i:]; then insert suffix into trie.

examples.trace: Length=5. i=0: S[0:] = 'hello'. i=1: S[1:] = 'ello'. i=2: S[2:] = 'llo'. i=3: S[3:] = 'lo'. i=4: S[4:] = 'o'. Total 5 suffixes.
Length=2. i=0: S[0:] = 'ab'. i=1: S[1:] = 'b'. Total 2 suffixes.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trie Node Insertion and Lookup
ladders.desc: Insert a character into a trie node or check if it exists.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves managing the children of a trie node. Each node in a trie represents a character (or part of a string) and has a mapping from characters to child nodes. The operation has two parts: lookup to check if a character is already a child, and insertion to add a new child if not present. Intuition: This allows the trie to build a tree where paths spell out strings, enabling efficient prefix checks. The algorithm for a single node: given a current node and a character c, access the children dictionary (e.g., a hash map). If c is in children, return the existing child node. If not, create a new TrieNode, add it to children under key c, and return the new node. This is typically done in constant time O(1) using hash maps. Space complexity is O(1) per operation, but accumulates as nodes are added. This feeds into L3 by being the atomic step when processing suffixes character-by-character. Common mistakes include not initializing the children dictionary or incorrectly updating the current node pointer. Real code pattern: In a TrieNode class, def get_or_create_child(self, char): if char not in self.children: self.children[char] = TrieNode(); return self.children[char].

examples.trace: Current node has children map: {'a': nodeA}. Check for 'b': not in children. Create new TrieNode for 'b'. Add to children: {'a': nodeA, 'b': newNode}. Return newNode.

examples.input: Node with children {'x': nodeX, 'y': nodeY}, char='x'
examples.trace: Children: {'x': nodeX, 'y': nodeY}. Check for 'x': present. Return nodeX without creation.

testCases.input: Node with multiple children, char new

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Substrings
ladders.desc: Define strings, substrings, and basic string operations for beginners.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. A string is a sequence of characters, such as 'apple' or '123'. In programming, strings are typically indexed starting from 0. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For example, in the string 'hello', substrings include 'h', 'he', 'hel', 'hell', 'hello', 'e', 'el', 'ell', 'ello', 'l', 'll', 'llo', 'l', 'lo', 'o'. The total number of substrings in a string of length n is n(n+1)/2, but this counts duplicates if characters repeat. The intuition for counting distinct substrings is to find how many unique sequences exist. Basic operations include accessing characters by index (e.g., S[0] for first character), slicing to get substrings (e.g., S[1:4] for characters from index 1 to 3), and iterating over characters. This foundation connects to L4 by providing the data (strings) that the trie operates on. Common mistakes include confusion between substrings (contiguous) and subsequences (not necessarily contiguous). Real code pattern: In Python, S = 'example'; substring = S[i:j] gets characters from i to j-1.

examples.trace: Length=3. Start index i=0: j=1:'c', j=2:'ca', j=3:'cat'. i=1: j=2:'a', j=3:'at'. i=2: j=3:'t'. Total 6 substrings.
Length=2. i=0: j=1:'o', j=2:'oo'. i=1: j=2:'o'. Total 3 substrings, but 'o' is duplicate.

approaches.name: Suffix Array with LCP O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Build a suffix array and compute the Longest Common Prefix (LCP) array. The total distinct substrings is calculated as n*(n+1)/2 minus the sum of LCP values. This leverages sorting and prefix comparisons for optimal performance.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Substrings Using Suffix Array with LCP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to count all unique substrings in a string using suffix array and LCP array.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages the combinatorial relationship between all possible substrings and repeated prefixes among suffixes. The total number of substrings in a string of length n is n*(n+1)/2. However, this counts duplicates. The key insight is that duplicate substrings appear as common prefixes between different suffixes. By building a suffix array (sorted list of all suffixes) and computing the Longest Common Prefix (LCP) array between consecutive suffixes in sorted order, we can subtract all repeated prefixes. The formula: distinct substrings = n*(n+1)/2 - sum(LCP). Intuition: Each LCP value represents the number of duplicate substrings contributed by that suffix pair. For example, if suffixes 'abc' and 'abcd' have LCP=3, they share substrings 'a', 'ab', 'abc' - these 3 are counted twice in n*(n+1)/2 but should be counted once. The algorithm: 1) Build suffix array O(n log n) using doubling algorithm with radix sort. 2) Compute LCP array O(n) using Kasai's algorithm. 3) Calculate total substrings minus sum of LCP. Time: O(n log n) dominated by suffix array construction. Space: O(n) for suffix array, rank arrays, and LCP. Connection to L1: This integrates the suffix array construction and LCP computation subroutines. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in LCP calculation, incorrect handling of empty string, not using stable sort for suffix array. Real code pattern: def count_distinct_substrings(s): n=len(s); suffix_array=build_suffix_array(s); lcp=build_lcp(s, suffix_array); total=n*(n+1)//2; return total-sum(lcp).

examples.trace: String: 'aba', n=3. Total substrings: 3*4/2=6. Build suffix array: suffixes: 'aba' (0), 'ba' (1), 'a' (2). Sorted: 'a' (2), 'aba' (0), 'ba' (1). LCP array: compare 'a' and 'aba': LCP=1 ('a'), compare 'aba' and 'ba': LCP=0. Sum LCP=1. Distinct=6-1=5. Substrings: 'a','b','ab','ba','aba' (5 unique).

String: 'aaaa', n=4. Total substrings: 4*5/2=10. Suffix array: all suffixes sorted: 'a' (3), 'aa' (2), 'aaa' (1), 'aaaa' (0). LCP: 'a' vs 'aa': LCP=1, 'aa' vs 'aaa': LCP=2, 'aaa' vs 'aaaa': LCP=3. Sum LCP=6. Distinct=10-6=4. Substrings: 'a','aa','aaa','aaaa' (4 unique).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Build Suffix Array with Doubling Algorithm
ladders.desc: Construct sorted array of all suffixes using prefix doubling with radix sort.
ladders.explanation: The suffix array is fundamental because sorted suffixes enable efficient LCP computation. The doubling algorithm builds the array in O(n log n) by sorting suffixes by increasingly longer prefixes. Intuition: Initially sort suffixes by first character (length-1 prefixes). Then, for k=1,2,4,... until covering entire string, sort by pairs (rank of current k-length prefix, rank of next k-length prefix). This works because the rank of a 2k-length prefix can be determined by ranks of its two k-length halves. Algorithm: 1) Initialize rank array for single characters. 2) For k from 1 to n (doubling each iteration): a) Create tuples (rank[i], rank[i+k] or -1 if i+k≥n). b) Radix sort these tuples. c) Update ranks based on sorted order. 3) Convert ranks to suffix indices. Time: O(n log n) - log n iterations, each with O(n) radix sort. Space: O(n) for ranks and temporary arrays. Connection to L0: This provides the sorted suffix array needed for LCP calculation. Common mistakes: Not using stable sort, incorrect handling of boundaries when i+k≥n, forgetting to update ranks properly. Real code pattern: def build_suffix_array(s): n=len(s); suffix=list(range(n)); rank=[ord(c) for c in s]; k=1; while k<n: suffix.sort(key=lambda i: (rank[i], rank[i+k] if i+k<n else -1)); new_rank=[0]*n; r=0; for i in range(n): if i>0 and (rank[suffix[i]]!=rank[suffix[i-1]] or rank[suffix[i]+k]!=rank[suffix[i-1]+k]): r+=1; new_rank[suffix[i]]=r; rank=new_rank; k*=2; return suffix.

examples.trace: String: 'aba', n=3. Initial ranks: a=97, b=98, a=97. k=1: suffixes indices [0,1,2]. Sort by (rank[i], rank[i+1]): (97,98) for i=0, (98,97) for i=1, (97,-1) for i=2. Sorted: (97,-1)→2, (97,98)→0, (98,97)→1. New ranks: index2→0, index0→1, index1→2. k=2: sort by (rank[i], rank[i+2]): (1,?) for i=0 (i+2=2≥3 so -1), (2,?) for i=1 (i+2=3≥3 so -1), (0,?) for i=2 (i+2=4≥3 so -1). Sorted: (0,-1)→2, (1,-1)→0, (2,-1)→1. Ranks unchanged. Final suffix array: [2,0,1] corresponding to suffixes: 'a' (idx2), 'aba' (idx0), 'ba' (idx1).

String: 'banana', n=6. Initial ranks: b=98, a=97, n=110, a=97, n=110, a=97. k=1: sort by first char: indices with a:1,3,5; n:2,4; b:0. Sorted: [1,3,5,2,4,0]. Update ranks: a's get 0,1,2; n's get 3,4; b gets 5. k=2: sort by (rank[i], rank[i+2]): pairs: (0,3) for i=1, (1,4) for i=3, (2,5) for i=5, (3,0) for i=2, (4,1) for i=4, (5,-1) for i=0. Sorted: (0,3)→1, (1,4)→3, (2,5)→5, (3,0)→2, (4,1)→4, (5,-1)→0. New order: [1,3,5,2,4,0]. Update ranks. Continue until k=4 (≥n/2). Final suffix array: [5,3,1,0,4,2] corresponding to suffixes: 'a','ana','anana','banana','na','nana'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute LCP Array from Suffix Array
ladders.desc: Calculate Longest Common Prefix between consecutive suffixes in sorted order using Kasai's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The LCP array is crucial for identifying duplicate substrings. Kasai's algorithm computes it in O(n) by leveraging the relationship between suffixes and their positions. Intuition: If we know LCP between suffix at position i and i+1 in suffix array, we can compute LCP for suffix at position i+1 and i+2 efficiently because when we move one character forward in the original string, the LCP decreases by at most 1. Algorithm: 1) Create inverse array rank where rank[pos] gives index in suffix array for suffix starting at pos. 2) Initialize lcp array of length n-1. 3) For each starting position i in original string, if rank[i]>0: a) Get j = suffix_array[rank[i]-1] (previous suffix in sorted order). b) While i+k<n and j+k<n and s[i+k]==s[j+k]: k++. c) lcp[rank[i]-1]=k. d) If k>0: k-- (for next iteration). Time: O(n) because k decreases at most n times total. Space: O(n) for rank and lcp arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the suffix array produced by the doubling algorithm. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in lcp indices, not decrementing k properly, incorrect rank array construction. Real code pattern: def build_lcp(s, suffix_array): n=len(s); rank=[0]*n; for i,sa in enumerate(suffix_array): rank[sa]=i; lcp=[0]*(n-1); k=0; for i in range(n): if rank[i]>0: j=suffix_array[rank[i]-1]; while i+k<n and j+k<n and s[i+k]==s[j+k]: k+=1; lcp[rank[i]-1]=k; if k>0: k-=1; return lcp.

examples.trace: String: 'aba', suffix_array=[2,0,1]. Build rank: rank[2]=0, rank[0]=1, rank[1]=2. Initialize lcp length 2, k=0. i=0: rank[0]=1>0, j=suffix_array[0]=2. Compare s[0]='a' vs s[2]='a': match, k=1. s[1]='b' vs s[3] out of bounds: stop. lcp[0]=1. k-- →0. i=1: rank[1]=2>0, j=suffix_array[1]=0. Compare s[1]='b' vs s[0]='a': no match, k=0. lcp[1]=0. k remains 0. i=2: rank[2]=0 not >0, skip. Final lcp: [1,0].

String: 'banana', suffix_array=[5,3,1,0,4,2]. Rank: [3,2,5,1,4,0]. lcp length 5, k=0. i=0: rank[0]=3>0, j=sa[2]=1. Compare s[0]='b' vs s[1]='a': no match, k=0, lcp[2]=0. i=1: rank[1]=2>0, j=sa[1]=3. Compare s[1]='a' vs s[3]='a': match k=1, s[2]='n' vs s[4]='n': match k=2, s[3]='a' vs s[5]='a': match k=3, s[4]='n' vs out: stop. lcp[1]=3, k=2. i=2: rank[2]=5>0, j=sa[4]=4. k=2: Compare s[2+2]='a' vs s[4+2] out: stop, adjust. Actually start from k=2: s[4]='n' vs s[6] out, so k reduces. Proper trace: i=2, j=4, k=2 initially from previous? Kasai uses k from previous iteration. After i=1, k=3 then k-- to 2. For i=2: j=4, compare s[2+2]='a' vs s[4+2] out, so k reduces to 0. lcp[4]=0. Continue... Final lcp: [1,3,0,0,2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sort Tuples with Radix Sort
ladders.desc: Stably sort array of integer tuples using least significant digit radix sort.
ladders.explanation: Radix sort is essential for the doubling algorithm's O(n log n) time. It sorts tuples (like (rank[i], rank[i+k])) stably in O(n) per digit. Intuition: Sort by least significant 'digit' (here tuple element) first, then move to more significant digits, preserving order from previous sorts (stable). For suffix arrays, we typically have 2-tuples. Algorithm: 1) For each tuple element from last to first: a) Count occurrences of each value. b) Compute prefix sums to determine positions. c) Place tuples in output array in stable order. 2) Repeat for all elements. Time: O(d * (n + range)) where d is number of tuple elements (2), range is max value (up to n). Since range is O(n), total O(n). Space: O(n) for count arrays and output. Connection to L2: This provides the sorting mechanism needed in suffix array construction. Common mistakes: Not stable sort, incorrect prefix sum calculation, mishandling negative values (use offset). Real code pattern: def radix_sort_tuples(tuples, max_val): n=len(tuples); output=[(0,0)]*n; for col in reversed(range(2)): count=[0]*(max_val+2); for t in tuples: count[t[col]+1]+=1; for i in range(1,len(count)): count[i]+=count[i-1]; for t in tuples: output[count[t[col]]]=t; count[t[col]]+=1; tuples=output[:]; return tuples.

examples.trace: Sort 2-tuples with radix sort. First sort by second element (col=1): Count: index0:0,1:2,2:2,3:0. Prefix: [0,0,2,4]. Place: (1,2)→pos count[2]=2, output[2]=(1,2), count[2]=3. (0,1)→pos count[1]=0, output[0]=(0,1), count[1]=1. (1,1)→pos count[1]=1, output[1]=(1,1), count[1]=2. (0,2)→pos count[2]=3, output[3]=(0,2), count[2]=4. Output: [(0,1),(1,1),(1,2),(0,2)]. Now sort by first element (col=0): Count:0:0,1:2,2:2. Prefix: [0,0,2,4]. Place: (0,1)→pos count[0]=0, output[0]=(0,1), count[0]=1. (1,1)→pos count[1]=2, output[2]=(1,1), count[1]=3. (1,2)→pos count[1]=3, output[3]=(1,2), count[1]=4. (0,2)→pos count[0]=1, output[1]=(0,2), count[0]=2. Final: [(0,1),(0,2),(1,1),(1,2)].

Sort by second element: Count:0:0,1:1,2:1,3:1,4:0. Prefix: [0,0,1,2,3]. Place: (2,0)→pos count[0]=0, output[0]=(2,0), count[0]=1. (1,3)→pos count[3]=3, output[3]=(1,3), count[3]=4. (2,1)→pos count[1]=1, output[1]=(2,1), count[1]=2. (0,2)→pos count[2]=2, output[2]=(0,2), count[2]=3. Output: [(2,0),(2,1),(0,2),(1,3)]. Sort by first element: Count:0:0,1:1,2:2,3:1. Prefix: [0,0,1,3,4]. Place: (2,0)→pos count[2]=3, output[3]=(2,0), count[2]=4. (2,1)→pos count[2]=4, output[4]=(2,1) but n=4, adjust. Actually: (2,0)→pos3, (2,1)→pos4 out of bounds? Correct: After first sort, output has 4 elements. For second sort: count based on first elements: value0:1 (from (0,2)), value1:1 (from (1,3)), value2:2 (from (2,0),(2,1)). Prefix: [0,0,1,2,4]. Place in reverse order of current list to maintain stability: (1,3)→pos count[1]=2, output[2]=(1,3), count[1]=3. (0,2)→pos count[0]=1, output[1]=(0,2), count[0]=2. (2,1)→pos count[2]=4, output[4]? Wait n=4, indices 0-3. Recompute: prefix [0,0,1,2,4]. Place: start from last element (1,3): value1→pos2, output[2]=(1,3), count[1]=3. Previous (0,2): value0→pos1, output[1]=(0,2), count[0]=2. Previous (2,1): value2→pos count[2]=4? Actually count[2] initially 2, after prefix gives starting index 2. So pos2, but output[2] taken, use count[2]=2, output[2]=(2,1), count[2]=3. Previous (2,0): value2→pos count[2]=3, output[3]=(2,0), count[2]=4. Final: [(0,2),(1,3),(2,1),(2,0)]? Not sorted. Let's do properly: Initial after first sort: [(2,0),(2,1),(0,2),(1,3)]. For second sort by first element: count occurrences: 0:1,1:1,2:2. Prefix: pos0:0, pos1:1, pos2:2, pos3:4. Now place from end to start: last (1,3) has first=1→pos prefix[1]=1, output[1]=(1,3), update prefix[1]=2. Next (0,2) first=0→pos prefix[0]=0, output[0]=(0,2), prefix[0]=1. Next (2,1) first=2→pos prefix[2]=2, output[2]=(2,1), prefix[2]=3. Next (2,0) first=2→pos prefix[2]=3, output[3]=(2,0), prefix[2]=4. Output: [(0,2),(1,3),(2,1),(2,0)] which is sorted by first element but not second. Actually radix sort should give final sorted by both: (0,2),(1,3),(2,0),(2,1). My trace has error. Simplified: Correct output is [(0,2),(1,3),(2,0),(2,1)].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare String Prefixes
ladders.desc: Determine the longest common prefix between two strings or substrings.
ladders.explanation: This operation is fundamental for LCP computation. It involves comparing characters from two starting positions until a mismatch or end is reached. Intuition: Two strings share a common prefix if their initial characters match consecutively. The length of this prefix indicates how many substrings they have in common. Algorithm: Given string s and indices i, j: 1) Initialize k=0. 2) While i+k < len(s) and j+k < len(s) and s[i+k] == s[j+k]: increment k. 3) Return k. Time: O(L) where L is length of common prefix, up to n. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used repeatedly in Kasai's algorithm to build LCP array. Common mistakes: Not checking array bounds, off-by-one errors, inefficient repeated comparisons. Real code pattern: def common_prefix(s, i, j): k=0; while i+k<len(s) and j+k<len(s) and s[i+k]==s[j+k]: k+=1; return k.

examples.trace: String: 'algorithm', i=0 (starts 'a'), j=3 (starts 'o'). Compare s[0]='a' vs s[3]='o': mismatch immediately. k=0. Return 0.

String: 'banana', i=1 (substring 'anana'), j=3 (substring 'ana'). Compare: k=0: s[1]='a' vs s[3]='a' match. k=1: s[2]='n' vs s[4]='n' match. k=2: s[3]='a' vs s[5]='a' match. k=3: s[4]='n' vs s[6] out of bounds (len=6). Stop. Return k=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Substrings and Prefixes
ladders.desc: Understand what substrings and prefixes are, and how to generate them.
ladders.explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For string of length n, total substrings = n*(n+1)/2 because you can choose start index i (0 to n-1) and end index j (i to n-1), giving sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (n-i) = n+(n-1)+...+1 = n(n+1)/2. A prefix is a special substring starting at index 0. For example, in 'abc', substrings: 'a','b','c','ab','bc','abc'. Prefixes: 'a','ab','abc'. Intuition: Every substring can be identified by its starting and ending positions. When comparing two suffixes (substrings from a position to end), their common prefix represents substrings that appear in both. This is key to counting distinct substrings: duplicates occur when different suffixes share prefixes. Algorithm to list all substrings: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): yield s[i:j+1]. Time: O(n³) to generate and store, but we only need count. Space: O(1) for count. Connection to L4: This foundational understanding leads to the need for prefix comparison operations. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequences (non-contiguous) with substrings, off-by-one in counting. Real code pattern: def count_total_substrings(n): return n*(n+1)//2.

examples.trace: String length n=2. Total substrings = 2*3/2=3. Generate: start i=0: j=0 → 'a', j=1 → 'ab'. start i=1: j=1 → 'b'. List: ['a','b','ab'].

approaches.name: Suffix Automaton O(n)
approaches.summary: Construct a suffix automaton (or suffix tree) for the string, where each state corresponds to a set of substrings. The number of distinct substrings is derived from the automaton's structure, enabling linear-time processing.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Substrings Using Suffix Automaton
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of suffix automaton to count all unique substrings in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: The suffix automaton is a finite automaton that recognizes all suffixes of a string, and by extension, all substrings. Each state represents a set of substrings that end at the same positions in the automaton. The key insight is that the number of distinct substrings equals the sum over all states of (length of longest string in state - length of link's longest string). This works because each distinct substring corresponds to a unique path in the automaton, and the difference in lengths accounts for new substrings introduced at each state. The algorithm processes the string character by character, extending the automaton. For each new character, we create a new state and update transitions and suffix links to maintain the automaton properties. The total distinct substrings are computed by summing (len[state] - len[link[state]]) for all states except the initial one. Time complexity is O(n) as each character addition takes amortized constant time due to suffix link jumps. Space complexity is O(n) for storing states and transitions. This feeds directly from L1 by integrating the state addition routine into the full construction. Common mistakes include incorrect suffix link updates or mishandling of clone states, which can be avoided by carefully following the extension rules. Real code pattern involves initializing with a start state, then iterating through characters to extend and update.

examples.trace: Initialize automaton with state 0 (len=0, link=-1). Process 'a': create state 1 (len=1, link=0), set trans[0]['a']=1. Distinct substrings = (1-0)=1. Process 'b': create state 2 (len=2, link=0), set trans[0]['b']=2, trans[1]['b']=2. Distinct substrings += (2-0)=2, total=3. Process 'c': create state 3 (len=3, link=0), set trans[0]['c']=3, trans[1]['c']=3, trans[2]['c']=3. Distinct substrings += (3-0)=3, total=6. Substrings: 'a','b','c','ab','bc','abc'.

Initialize state 0 (len=0, link=-1). Process 'a': state 1 (len=1, link=0), trans[0]['a']=1. Distinct=1. Process 'b': state 2 (len=2, link=0), trans[0]['b']=2, trans[1]['b']=2. Distinct+=2, total=3. Process 'a': find p=2, set q=trans[2]['a']=None, create state 3 (len=3). Set trans[2]['a']=3. Since p's link is 0 and trans[0]['a'] exists as 1, set link[3]=1. Update some transitions. Distinct += (3-1)=2, total=5. Substrings: 'a','b','ab','ba','aba'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Adding a Character to Suffix Automaton
ladders.desc: Extend the suffix automaton by one character, updating states and links.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine adds a new character to the automaton, which is the core operation in building it incrementally. The intuition is to maintain the invariant that after processing each prefix, the automaton recognizes all suffixes of that prefix. When adding character c, we create a new state cur with length = last.length + 1. We then iterate from the last state, adding transitions for c to cur until we find a state p that already has a transition for c. If no such state exists, we set link[cur] to the initial state. If p exists and its transition leads to a state q with length = p.length + 1, we set link[cur] = q. Otherwise, we clone q to create a new state clone, copy its transitions, set its length = p.length + 1, and update links so that link[q] = link[cur] = clone. This ensures the automaton remains minimal. The time complexity per addition is amortized O(1) due to suffix link jumps, and space is O(1) per state added. This feeds into L0 as the building block for constructing the full automaton. Common mistakes include forgetting to update transitions for all relevant states or incorrect clone handling, avoided by following the algorithm strictly. Real code pattern involves loops over suffix links and condition checks.

examples.trace: Start with automaton for 'a': state 0 (len=0, link=-1), state 1 (len=1, link=0), trans[0]['a']=1. Set last=1. Add 'b': create cur=2 (len=2). Set p=1, since trans[1]['b'] is None, set trans[1]['b']=2. Update p=link[1]=0. Since trans[0]['b'] is None, set trans[0]['b']=2. Since p=-1, set link[2]=0. Last becomes 2.

Automaton for 'ab': states 0,1,2 as above. Last=2. Add 'a': create cur=3 (len=3). Set p=2, trans[2]['a'] is None, set trans[2]['a']=3. Update p=link[2]=0. trans[0]['a'] exists as 1, so q=1. Since len[1]=1 and len[0]+1=1, set link[3]=1. Last becomes 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Suffix Links and State Minimization
ladders.desc: Understand how suffix links connect states to minimize the automaton.
ladders.explanation: Suffix links are pointers from a state to another state that represents the longest suffix of the current state's strings that appears elsewhere in the automaton. This is the central technique for achieving linear time. The intuition is that by following suffix links, we can efficiently find where to add new transitions without scanning the entire string. Each state has a suffix link to a state whose longest string is the longest proper suffix of the current state's strings. This creates a tree of links rooted at the initial state. Minimization occurs because states are merged if they share the same set of ending positions for their strings. When adding a character, if a transition already exists, we may need to clone a state to split it, ensuring that each state's strings have consistent lengths. This reduces the number of states to O(n). Time complexity benefits from amortized analysis as suffix link jumps reduce redundant work. Space is O(n) for links. This feeds into L1 by providing the logic for updating links during character addition. Common mistakes include setting incorrect link values or not cloning when necessary, avoided by checking length conditions. Real code pattern involves comparing lengths of states during updates.

examples.trace: States: 0 (len=0, link=-1), 1 (len=1, link=0 for 'a'), 2 (len=2, link=0 for 'ab'). For state 2, longest string 'ab', its longest proper suffix is 'b', which is represented by state 0 (since 'b' is not a full state yet, but the initial state handles empty string). Thus link[2]=0.
States: 0 (len=0, link=-1), 1 (len=1, link=0 for 'a'), 2 (len=2, link=1 for 'aa'). For state 2, longest string 'aa', its longest proper suffix is 'a', represented by state 1. Thus link[2]=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — States and Transitions in Automaton
ladders.desc: Define states and transitions to represent sets of substrings.
ladders.explanation: In a suffix automaton, each state is a node that represents a set of substrings that end at the same positions in the string. The intuition is that by grouping substrings with common endings, we can avoid redundant representation and achieve efficiency. A state has: a length (the length of the longest substring in the set), a suffix link (pointer to another state), and a map of transitions to other states via characters. Transitions indicate how to extend the current substring by one character. For example, from a state representing substrings ending at certain positions, a transition on character 'c' leads to a state representing those substrings plus 'c'. This building block is intermediate because it sets up the data structure needed for the core logic. Time and space are O(1) per state or transition, but overall O(n). This feeds into L2 by providing the components that suffix links connect. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization of states or missing transitions, avoided by defining clear structures. Real code pattern involves classes or dictionaries for states.

examples.trace: State 0: initial, represents empty string. State 1: represents 'a'. State 2: represents 'ab' and 'b'. Transitions: from 0, 'a' goes to 1, 'b' goes to 2; from 1, 'b' goes to 2.
State 0: initial. State 1: represents 'a'. State 2: represents 'aa'. Transitions: from 0, 'a' goes to 1; from 1, 'a' goes to 2.

testCases.expected: States: 0,1,2,3; Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 0->2 on 'b', 0->3 on 'c', 1->2 on 'b', 1->3 on 'c', 2->3 on 'c'
States: 0,1,2,3; Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 0->2 on 'b', 1->2 on 'b', 1->3 on 'a', 2->3 on 'a'
States: 0,1,2,3,4; Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'a', 2->3 on 'a', 3->4 on 'a'
States: 0,1,2,3; Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 1->2 on 'a', 1->3 on 'b', 2->3 on 'b'
States: 0,1,2,3; Transitions: 0->1 on 'a', 0->2 on 'b', 1->2 on 'b', 1->3 on 'b', 2->3 on 'b'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Suffixes of a String
ladders.desc: Process all suffixes of a string efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves generating or traversing all suffixes of a given string, which is essential for building a suffix automaton because the automaton must recognize every suffix. The intuition is that by handling suffixes incrementally, we can construct the automaton in linear time. For a string of length n, suffixes are substrings starting at each position i (0 to n-1) and going to the end. For example, for 'abc', suffixes are 'abc', 'bc', 'c'. In the automaton construction, we add characters one by one, and after adding each character, the current prefix's suffixes are implicitly handled via suffix links. This operation is basic because it underpins the incremental update process. Time complexity is O(n) to iterate through all suffixes if done naively, but in the automaton, it's optimized using links. Space is O(1) per iteration. This feeds into L3 by providing the sequence of steps for state updates. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in suffix indices or missing the empty suffix, avoided by careful indexing. Real code pattern involves loops from 0 to n-1.

examples.trace: For i=0: suffix from index 0 is 'ab'. For i=1: suffix from index 1 is 'b'. Total 2 suffixes.
For i=0: 'aba'. For i=1: 'ba'. For i=2: 'a'. Total 3 suffixes.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Identify all possible substrings of a given string.
ladders.explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. This is the simplest prerequisite concept because counting distinct substrings is the core problem. The intuition is that for a string of length n, there are n*(n+1)/2 total substrings if duplicates are counted, but we want unique ones. For example, in 'abc', substrings are 'a', 'b', 'c', 'ab', 'bc', 'abc'. To generate all substrings, we consider all starting indices i from 0 to n-1 and all ending indices j from i to n-1, yielding the substring from i to j inclusive. This foundation is essential for understanding why the problem is hard (naive enumeration is O(n²)) and how the suffix automaton optimizes it. Time complexity for naive generation is O(n³) if we consider string building, but conceptually O(n²) substrings. Space is O(n) per substring if stored. This feeds into L4 by providing the set of strings that suffixes are a subset of. Common mistakes include missing single-character substrings or counting non-contiguous sequences, avoided by ensuring contiguity. Real code pattern involves nested loops over i and j.

examples.trace: Start i=0: j=0 gives 'a', j=1 gives 'ab'. Start i=1: j=1 gives 'b'. Total 3 substrings.
Start i=0: j=0 gives 'a', j=1 gives 'aa'. Start i=1: j=1 gives 'a'. Total 3 substrings, but distinct are 'a' and 'aa'.
